food_subgroup: Braised or stewed beef dishes served as a set meal with rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a beef brisket food product class but no specific entry for stewed beef brisket with tomato as a set meal. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers beef meat dishes as a category. No specific code exists for stewed beef brisket with tomato set meal; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 is the closest parent for a beef dish. No specific code for stewed beef brisket with tomato set meal exists.

serving.description: 1 set meal plate (beef brisket with tomato over steamed rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving consists of approximately 150 g of stewed beef brisket with tomato sauce served over approximately 200 g of steamed white rice, with a small side, totalling around 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, brisket, braised / stewed with tomato-based sauce over steamed white rice — composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) — component-based estimation; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for comparable Chinese beef stew dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish as a set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for braised beef brisket (USDA FDC ID 174032, beef brisket braised) and cooked white rice (USDA FDC ID 169756), weighted to reflect the approximate composition of the set meal (approximately 33% beef brisket with tomato sauce, 44% steamed rice, 23% sauce and vegetables). The composite per-100 g values are derived from this weighted blend. HPB Singapore nutrient tables for Chinese braised beef dishes were used as a plausibility cross-check.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed beef-rice dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stewed beef brisket with tomato set meal. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant glycemic driver being steamed white rice (GI approximately 64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice) diluted by the protein and fat content of the beef brisket component, which attenuates the overall glycemic response. A mixed beef-and-rice dish GI of approximately 50–58 is consistent with published analogous estimates in the University of Sydney GI Database for rice-based mixed dishes with protein. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 78.75) / 100 ≈ 42.5, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving ≈ 78.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single set meal serving may contribute approximately 1,395 mg of sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load driven by steamed white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the steamed white rice in this set meal contributes a moderate glycemic load of approximately 42.5 per serving; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion can meaningfully lower postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from beef brisket, and high sodium from braising sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to its saturated fat content from beef brisket and elevated sodium from soy-based braising sauce; choosing leaner cuts or smaller beef portions and requesting low-sodium sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stewed Beef Brisket with Tomato Set (番茄炖牛腩套餐) is a popular Chinese comfort food dish served as a complete set meal in Singapore rice kitchen and bento-style restaurants. The dish features beef brisket — a flavourful, collagen-rich cut from the lower chest of the cow — slow-braised or stewed for an extended period (typically 1.5 to 2.5 hours) in a savoury tomato-based sauce seasoned with soy sauce, ginger, garlic, star anise, and sugar. The prolonged stewing renders the brisket tender and gelatinous, with the tomato providing natural acidity and sweetness that balances the richness of the beef. The set meal is typically served over a generous portion of steamed white jasmine rice, which absorbs the deeply flavoured braising sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, protein-rich meal providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful iron, zinc, and B12 content from the beef. The sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce in the braising liquid, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load is moderate, driven primarily by the steamed rice component. Clinically, this dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but warrants caution for those with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, and glycemic load profile. It is a staple of Cantonese-influenced Chinese home cooking and is widely available in Singapore's hawker centres, food courts, and casual dining establishments.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact set meal dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for braised beef brisket and cooked white rice, weighted by approximate set meal composition, and cross-checked against HPB Singapore nutrient tables for comparable Chinese beef stew dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for white rice and mixed rice-protein dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central braised beef brisket (FDC ID 174032) and cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756), weighted at approximately 33% beef with sauce, 44% rice, and 23% tomato sauce and vegetables by mass. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because they were not consistently reported or reliably estimable from the USDA FoodData Central component data used for this composite dish estimate: monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; potassium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; calcium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; cholesterol_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data; water_g is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data. 3. GI is an analogous estimate of 54 based on University of Sydney GI Database values for boiled white rice and published mixed beef-rice dish GI data; the protein and fat from beef brisket attenuate the glycemic response relative to plain rice. 4. Halal status is marked false as beef brisket preparation method and slaughter certification cannot be confirmed for all serving restaurants; individual restaurant halal certification should be verified. 5. The set meal price range of SGD 13.10–19.70 is preserved from the GrabFood Singapore source record for commercial context only and does not affect nutritional values.
